schools.summary: Ellicott: the verb means 'mustered'; David long at Mahanaim organising the gathering forces; Clarke gives the campaign overview.
Henry: David prayed before he counted; good order and conduct serve as well as numbers; Providence kept him off the field.
Gill: the host was about four thousand per Josephus, though the sequel suggests more.
Clarke: David's small company had been greatly recruited, though its exact number cannot be told.

sides.position: about four thousand, per Josephus
perhaps ten thousand, reading v.3 as 'we are ten thousand strong'

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D: the threefold division was common Israelite practice; David proposed to take the chief command himself.
Gill: Joab's third was likely the centre, since he was general-in-chief.

themes: the king would lead in person

schools.summary: Ellicott: emend 'now' to 'thou' with LXX/Vulg./Chaldee; David is the centre of the cause and better kept as a reserve.
Clarke: the particle 'now' (attah) is a mistake for the pronoun 'thou' (attah), as the versions read.
Gill: the people were as resolute as David, overruling their king for his own good.

debates.question: How should the clause about 'ten thousand' be read?

sides.position: 'thou art worth ten thousand of us' (emended text, following the versions)
'now there are ten thousand like us' (received text, basis for a larger army)

schools.summary: Gill: David's yielding was great condescension and prudence, since the people's counsel sprang from loyalty.
Ellicott: David was nothing loth to avoid the encounter, yet reviewed the troops and charged them tenderly for 'the young man.'
Henry: it is wisdom to be overruled for our good; Providence kept David off the field lest his tenderness spare Absalom.
Clarke and Wesley: David feels as a father; the charge springs from indulgence, from his sense of being the cause, from Absalom's youth, and from piety.
Henry, with Bishop Hall: an 'ill-placed mercy,' yet a type of the true King who prayed 'Father, forgive them.'
K&D render it bluntly: 'Mildly for me with the boy Absalom,' heard by all the captains.

debates.question: Is David's charge blamable weakness or a shadow of divine mercy?

sides.position: an injurious, excessive tenderness in a father and king
a type of God's immeasurable mercy toward rebels

themes: David the cause of the rebellion

schools.summary: A genuine geographical debate over where the 'wood of Ephraim' lay; Ellicott places it east of Jordan, K&D west, in Ephraim's forested inheritance.
Gill: Josephus called it a great field with a wood behind it, the terrain that shaped the coming rout.

sides.position: east of the Jordan, despite the name (from the movements of both armies)
west of the Jordan, in Ephraim's forested inheritance (Joshua 17:15-16)

schools.summary: Ellicott, Clarke, K&D weigh the figure of 20,000, cautioning that 'that day' may cover the whole campaign; the prestige of law and order aided David.
Gill: Absalom's people were beaten before the servants of David — a slaughter of David's own subjects.
Henry: the rebels smarted justly for treason against the Lord's anointed, yet the victory wounded the kingdom's own body.
Clarke and Wesley: the wood killed by pits, swamps, weariness, the pursuing sword — and, per the Chaldee/Syriac/Arabic, by wild beasts.
K&D: the region was full of ravines, precipices, and marshes into which the flying foe perished.
Gill: the broken ranks fled in confusion all over the field and into the neighbouring wood.
Henry: they destroyed themselves by their flight; what refuge can rebels find from divine vengeance?

debates.question: How did 'the wood devour more than the sword'?

sides.position: wild beasts of the forest (the Chaldee, Syriac, Arabic versions)
pits, swamps, ravines, and the pursuing sword

themes: the wood devoured more than the sword

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D are skeptical of the hair: the text says only that his head was caught fast between the boughs; the hair-legend stems from Josephus.
Wesley: entangled by his long hair, the matter of his pride made the instrument of his ruin; Clarke: caught by the neck in a fork, nearly dead already.
Henry: hung between heaven and earth, abandoned of both; God 'created a new thing' to mark so uncommon a criminal.
Gill: fleeing in terror he ran toward David's men, who let him pass, mindful of the king's charge.

debates.question: Was Absalom held by his hair or his neck?

sides.position: by his long hair — the emblem of his pride
by his head/neck jammed in a fork; the text does not say hair

schools.summary: K&D: the man reports; Joab asks why he did not strike, promising silver and a girdle.
Clarke: the intervals of time imply Absalom was nearly dead, past recovery even if taken down.
Gill: the man was one of David's soldiers, who found him in his helpless posture.
Henry: Absalom now a spectacle and a mark, none of his courted friends at hand to relieve him.
K&D: Joab openly declares a bounty for the killing the king had forbidden.
Clarke: the girdle was a soldier's chief ornament and a rich gift (Jonathan to David, Ajax to Hector).
Gill: Joab turns from the report to reprove the very obedience the man had shown.
Henry: the reward signifies a captain's commission — promotion offered for a forbidden blow.
Gill: the soldier spoke 'disdaining' Joab's proposal — scorning a bribe of any size against the king's son.
Ellicott and K&D: a textual choice between 'mine own life' (margin, English, Vulg.) and 'his life' (LXX, text); the parley exposes Joab's unscrupulous character.
Clarke: a strong appeal to Joab's loyalty, against which he was 'proof' — deaf to every generous sentiment.
Gill: the soldier would have wronged his own conscience and life, or taken Absalom's, against the king's word.
Henry: 'Those that love the treason hate the traitor' — Joab would have prosecuted the deed he now urges.

debates.question: Whose 'life' would the deed have wronged?

sides.position: the soldier's own life (margin; English; Vulgate)
Absalom's life, taken by the blow (LXX; text)

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D: the 'darts' were staffs snatched in haste; 'heart' is the same word as 'midst'; it was a distinct disobedience of orders — deliberate murder.
Clarke: Absalom's life was quadruply forfeited to the law, yet Joab's act was a base, cowardly murder.
Henry: exquisite torment while yet alive — was this to 'deal gently'? — yet Salus populi suprema lex; real service to king and country.
Gill: Joab resolves to do the deed himself rather than wait on the reluctant soldier.

debates.question: Was Joab's killing of Absalom justified or murder?

sides.position: necessary service to the state — it ended the rebellion
wilful, deliberate murder in defiance of the king's charge

schools.summary: K&D: ten of Joab's armour-bearers compassed Absalom and slew him, then Joab stayed the pursuit.
Wesley: the darts had not dispatched him, so they smote him again and killed him.
Gill: the ten were Joab's own armour-bearers, acting by his orders.
Henry: they made a ring about him in triumph and slew him — 'So let all thy enemies perish, O Lord.'
Ellicott: the trumpet parallels 2 Samuel 2:28 and 20:22; with Absalom dead the rebellion was over. K&D: Joab wished to spare the people.
Clarke: the rebellion extinguished, Joab would not slaughter the 'deluded people.'
Gill: the trumpet was the sign of a retreat.
Henry: no more blood, no prisoners — they are all the king's good subjects again.
K&D: an ignominious cairn like those over Achan (Joshua 7:26) and the king of Ai (Joshua 8:29); Clarke on cairns; Ellicott on 'every one to his tent.'
Gill: buried in the wood of Ephraim, on the very ground of his overthrow.
Henry: the heap signified he ought to have been stoned as a rebellious son (Deuteronomy 21:21); his beauty and projects perish with him.
K&D and Ellicott: the king's dale is the valley of Shaveh (Genesis 14:17), probably near Jerusalem; Clarke notes the 'Absalom's pillar' shown today is modern.
Wesley: he reared it to preserve his name, when oblivion would have been more for his honour.
Gill: Absalom self-devised the scheme in his lifetime to perpetuate his memory.
Henry: 'written childless' though once he had sons (2 Samuel 14:27); a rude heap replaces his marble — those that exalt themselves shall be abased.

sides.position: near Jerusalem, in the valley of the Kidron
uncertain — some place it near Sodom

schools.summary: K&D: Ahimaaz would proclaim that the Lord had avenged the king, but Joab judged him 'no man of good tidings to-day.'
Gill: Ahimaaz, son of Zadok, had stayed with the army after carrying Hushai's message.
Henry: Ahimaaz ran from love, not reward; Joab knew the death would spoil the acceptableness of all the rest.
K&D and Ellicott: the word is used chiefly of good tidings; Joab, knowing how painful the news would be, forbade Ahimaaz to bear it.
Gill: Ahimaaz being a good man and a priest's son, Joab spared him an errand that would not commend him.
Henry: such tidings are 'fitter to be brought by a footman than by a priest.'
Gill canvasses the Cushite's identity — Ethiopian proselyte, or a dark-complexioned Israelite, perhaps Cush the Benjaminite.
Ellicott: 'the Cushite,' probably an Ethiopian slave in Joab's service, expendable if he met the king's displeasure; K&D: he bowed and ran.
Clarke: the death was not yet public; Joab had told Cushi privately, so Ahimaaz could not tell whether Absalom was dead.

sides.position: an Ethiopian (Cushite) slave in Joab's service
an Israelite of dark complexion, perhaps Cush the Benjaminite

themes: the full truth entrusted to a slave

schools.summary: K&D: 'let whatever will happen'; Ahimaaz outran the Cushite. Ellicott: 'no tidings ready' means no message sufficient for a special runner.
Gill: he could not be easy though a messenger was sent, but pressed Joab still.
Henry: perhaps vanity in his running, perhaps tenderness to prepare the king gradually — Henry holds both.
Ellicott: 'the way of the plain' means the circuit/surrounding country (Nehemiah 12:28) — a longer but smoother road, so Ahimaaz outran the Cushite; K&D: David sat between the gates.
Gill: being importunate, Ahimaaz was granted leave to run.
Gill: Mahanaim had two walls and two gates; David sat between them waiting for news.
Clarke: David in the seat of justice, a sentinel on the turret; Wesley: the double gates for security, the king seated to hear tidings.
K&D: the two gates are the outer and inner city gate, with a court between where the king waited.
Henry: David stayed at Mahanaim on the border, sitting in the gate expecting to hear.
K&D: a lone runner means good news, for a flight would have shown many fugitives.
Clarke: 'if he be alone, there is tidings' — that is, good tidings; a lost battle scatters men in all directions.
Gill: the watchman called from the tower to the king seated between the gates.
K&D: a second lone runner, also read as a herald; the watchman recognises Ahimaaz's running.
Gill: the watchman saw another man running, at a further distance.
Henry: Ahimaaz so good a man that his message is presumed good; a wish that gospel tidings came by good men.
K&D: recognised by his swiftness; Joab would not have sent so good a man with bad news.
Gill: 'me thinketh' means 'I see, I perceive; so it appears to me.'
Henry: Ahimaaz known by his running and reputed good; the king's sound reasoning is about to fail him.
K&D and Ellicott: 'Shalom!' — Peace — the eager cry, then a reverent prostration and a distinct announcement of the victory.
Henry: as a priest he gives God the glory; blessing God prepares David for 'the approaching news of its allay.'
Gill: he called out with a loud voice as soon as he came within hearing.
K&D: Ahimaaz spoke as if sent before the fate was known; Ellicott: he 'skilfully, though untruthfully, evades the question.'
Clarke: probably Ahimaaz did not know of the death; others think he knew and told an untruth to spare David.
Gill: David's question shows he cared more for Absalom than for the army's success.
Henry: Ahimaaz left the matter doubtful, referring the king to the next messenger, lest the blow fall too suddenly.

debates.question: Did Ahimaaz not know of Absalom's death, or did he dissemble?

sides.position: he honestly did not yet know the outcome
he knew and gently evaded, to spare the king

themes: a father who forgets he is a king

schools.summary: K&D: 'Turn, and stand here,' that he might hear the further news from the Cushite.
Clarke: David meant to confront two messengers and compare their accounts.
Gill: Ahimaaz to stand by until the other came, that David might learn the true state from both.
Henry: Ahimaaz, having delivered his message, refers the king to the next runner for a fuller account.
K&D: the Cushite proclaims that the Lord has procured the king justice out of the hand of all who rose against him.
Gill: the Cushite came 'a little after' the swifter Ahimaaz.

themes: the Lord has avenged the king

schools.summary: K&D: the death is 'indicated clearly enough' in the courteous wish; Ellicott: the Cushite answers plainly, not appreciating the king's feelings.
Gill: David repeats the question, showing what lay nearest his heart.
Clarke: this fixation on Absalom merited Joab's later reproof (2 Samuel 19:5).
K&D: paternal weakness and anger at Joab blind David to Absalom's guilt and to God's penal justice; Ellicott: grief sharpened by his own sin, yet 'criminally weak.'
Henry: blamable for over-fond love and quarrelling with divine justice, yet a shadow of the Saviour who died for his enemies.
Clarke hopes Absalom may have implored mercy while hanging; Wesley thinks the cry rather the effect of strong, unadvised passion.
Gill: the king was greatly troubled, his affections and passions stirred.

debates.question: Did David's grief spring from concern for Absalom's soul, or from blamable passion?

sides.position: a tender hope for Absalom's eternal state
the effect of excessive passion and paternal weakness

themes: 'would God I had died for thee'
